title: Switching between oversight and support mode: the duality of care culture in Russian non-selective universities
abstract: The study examines how care forms university culture. The study draws on 135 interviews with students, faculty, and administration from four non-selective universities in the Russian periphery. The study reveals the duality of care practiced and perceived in these universities. On the one hand, university staff act as supportive mentors, accommodating students' personal and academic needs. On the other hand, university staff practice excessive supervision of students who are treated as dependent and incompetent, while parental involvement limits their autonomy. These universities maintain a school-like environment, prioritising moral education over academic rigor. We interpret the findings not only through the duality of care, but also through the lens of inherited and socially constructed female roles that influence the work and practices of faculty and administrators.
authors_text: Akuneeva, Tatiana; Maslova, Ekaterina; Romanenko, Ksenia; Platonova, Daria
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
